topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the blunt force of a dropped book. My stomach plummeted. I clicked it open, and the headline screamed my name, followed by the byline of a complete stranger. A wave of heat rushed up my neck, making my palms instantly slick. I reread the text, my eyes skipping over the familiar phrasing, searching for a slip-up, a misplaced sentence that would somehow explain this monumental oversight. When I finally found it, the reality settled in: someone else had taken my words, my analysis, and published them as their own. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my apartment, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest. I wanted to smash my laptop screen, to erase the entire digital footprint of this blunder.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, a slow, cold dread creeping through my limbs. The publication date mocked me. My carefully crafted piece, the one I’d poured weeks into, was undeniably there, but the author listed was someone whose name felt like a physical slap. A strange tightness seized my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. I started typing furiously, drafting a response, but the words felt clumsy and inadequate. Every keystroke was heavy, weighted down by the sheer awkwardness of the situation. I kept glancing at the door, half-expecting someone to walk in and witness this public misattribution.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, I froze mid-sip of my coffee. The steam curled around my face, momentarily obscuring the stark text. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscle vibrating beneath my skin. It wasn't just a mistake; it was a complete erasure of ownership. A flush spread across my cheeks, warm and undeniable. I slowly lowered the mug, the ceramic cool against my fingertips. I began to rub my temples, a desperate attempt to physically ward off the sudden, intense pressure building behind my eyes. The air in the room suddenly felt too thick, too heavy to move through.
